The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]][[Category:-inline|Tsukazaki Kofun Group}} *[https://kimotsuki-town.jp/soshiki/rekishiminzoku/1/2/1650.html Kimotsuki Town home page] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.kagoshima-kankou.com/guide/10373 Kagoshima Tourism Web] {{in lang|ja}}

Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11428440]] Wikidata name: Tsukazaki Kofun Group [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg; File:Tukazaki b (Kimotsuki).jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|31.340278|N|130.968333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kimotsuki|qid=Q1350770}}| region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|qid=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11428440]] Wikidata name: Tsukazaki Kofun Group [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg; File:Tukazaki b (Kimotsuki).jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|31.340278|N|130.968333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kimotsuki|WD=Q1350770}}| region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)]]

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11428440]] Wikidata name: Tsukazaki Kofun Group [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg; File:Tukazaki b (Kimotsuki).jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|31.340278|N|130.968333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kimotsuki|WD=Q1350770}}| region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11428440]] Wikidata name: Tsukazaki Kofun Group [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg; File:Tukazaki b (Kimotsuki).jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|31.340278|N|130.968333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kimotsuki|WD=Q1350770}}| region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11428440]] Wikidata name: Tsukazaki Kofun Group [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg; File:Tukazaki b (Kimotsuki).jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|31.340278|N|130.968333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kimotsuki|WD=Q1350770}}| region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)]]

The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11428440]] Wikidata name: Tsukazaki Kofun Group [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg; File:Tukazaki b (Kimotsuki).jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|31.340278|N|130.968333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Kimotsuki|WD=Q1350770}}| region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of [[:en:Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|Kimotsuki]], [[:en:Kagoshima Prefecture|Kagoshima Prefecture]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the [[:en:Ōsumi Peninsula|Ōsumi area]] around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a [[:en:Camphora officinarum|''Kusunoki'']] estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the [[:en:Shimazu clan|Shimazu clan]], and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by [[:en:Typhoon Yancy (1993)|Typhoon No. 13]] in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from [[:en:Shibushi Station|Shibushi Station]] on the [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Nichinan Line|Nichinan Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from {{ill|Shibushi Station|en|Shibushi Station|WD=Q7496088|lt=Shibushi Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nichinan Line|en|Nichinan Line|WD=Q871676|lt=Nichinan Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|WD=Q6573269|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukazaki Kofun Group]][[en:Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster]][[ja:塚崎古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11428440]] Wikidata name: Tsukazaki Kofun Group [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Infobox ancient site}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | native_name =塚崎古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg; File:Tukazaki b (Kimotsuki).jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tsukazaki Kofun No.51 | map_type = Japan#Japan Kagoshima Prefecture | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{Coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E|region:JP-46_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}}; {{Coord|31.340278|N|130.968333|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location ={{ill|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|en|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|WD=Q1350770|lt=Kimotsuki, Kagoshima}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}; {{ill|Kimotsuki|WD=Q1350770}}| region = [[Kyushu]] | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th-5th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|20|11.40|N|130|58|2.96|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster'''|塚崎古墳群}} is a group of [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofuns]], located in the town of {{ill|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|en|Kimotsuki, Kagoshima|WD=Q1350770|lt=Kimotsuki}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus cluster was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1945, with the area under protection expanded in 2013, 2020 and 2024.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2996|title=塚崎古墳群|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
==Overview== The Tsukazaki Kofun Cluster consists of five {{nihongo|''[[Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, and 39 {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} round tumuli. The cluster is located on the Shirasu Plateau and Tsukasaki Plateau at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters on the right bank of the Kimomi River at the northern foot of the Kunimi Mountain Range. It is located about three kilometers east of Kimotsuki Town Hall. More than ten underground horizontal cave tombs have been confirmed, in which the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is dug horizontally from the bottom of a vertical pit. This design was popular in the {{ill|Ōsumi Peninsula|en|Ōsumi Peninsula|WD=Q386078|lt=Ōsumi area}} around the 5th century. Therefore, it is thought that the entire group of tombs was constructed between the 4th and 5th centuries. However, no [[archaeological excavation]]s have been performed on many of the tumuli in the cluster, including all of the keyhole-shaped tumulus, and so much remains unknown.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Kofun No.39, also known as the "Hanamure Kofun" is the southernmost ''zenpō-kōen-fun'' in Japan. It has a total length of approximately 66 meters and a height of ten meters, and was constructed by shaping the tip of a small natural hill. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' has been found on top of the mound. Kofun No. 25 contains a small round-bottom pot and earthenware stand that is presumed to have been used for burial rituals. Kofun No. 18 contained similar pot-shaped clay pottery, and Kofun No. 31 had {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} thought to have been obtained from the [[:en:Kinai region|Kinai region]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> File:Tsukazaki no Kusu, zenkei-2.jpg|Kofun No.1 with Tsukazaki Kusu tree File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-10, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.10 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-11, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.11 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-21, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.21 File:Tsukazaki Kofun Group-51, funkyu.jpg|Kofun No.51 </gallery>
On the mound of Kofun No. 1 is the {{nihongo|Tsukazaki Kusu tree|塚崎のクス||}}, a {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=''Kusunoki''}} estimated to be approximately 1,300 years old. This area is within the precincts of Otsuka Shrine, which was founded by [[Shimazu Tadahisa]], the founder of the {{ill|Shimazu clan|en|Shimazu clan|WD=Q1061406|lt=Shimazu clan}}, and it is said that it was considered a sacred tree from the time the shrine was founded. It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by {{ill|Typhoon Yancy (1993)|en|Typhoon Yancy (1993)|WD=Q17061365|lt=Typhoon No. 13}} in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from {{ill|Shibushi Station|en|Shibushi Station|WD=Q7496088|lt=Shibushi Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nichinan Line|en|Nichinan Line|WD=Q871676|lt=Nichinan Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)|WD=Q6573269|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kagoshima)}}
==References== {{reflist}}

It measures 25 meters in height and 14 meters in circumference, and was once at least ten meters taller, but was damaged by {{ill|Typhoon Yancy (1993)|en|Typhoon Yancy (1993)|WD=Q17061365|lt=Typhoon No. 13}} in 1993. In addition, the tree's vigor has been declining in recent years, with rot seen in various parts of the tree and some parts of the trunk becoming hollow. The tree is a [[Monuments of Japan|National Natural Monument]].<ref name="Shiseki"/><ref name="Bunka2">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/173555|title=塚崎のクス |language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
The site is approximately 39 minutes by car from {{ill|Shibushi Station|en|Shibushi Station|WD=Q7496088|lt=Shibushi Station}} on the {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} {{ill|Nichinan Line|en|Nichinan Line|WD=Q871676|lt=Nichinan Line}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
